metadata.title: Report Claim Tracing: Read for Evidence, Not Flow

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Namaste, Liam — in for Bear. This is Liam, in for Bear? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: This is Liam, in for Bear?
props.segment: Report Claim Tracing: Read for Evidence,

beats.narration_text: He asked Cowork to assemble a grant progress report from the approved source folder. Twelve pages arrived. Professional structure. Clear narrative. He read for flow and approved it. Two weeks later, a program officer flagged a number: 'exceeded projections' in the executive summary. The source document contained a projection, not a result. Cowork had assembled the narrative correctly. It had not noticed the category error. He had not caught it either. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, assemble grant progress report from approved source folder

props.command: Assemble a grant progress report from this approved source folder

beats.narration_text: Cowork synthesizes — it selects, compresses, and smooths. Caveats drop first. Projected results migrate toward reported results. Discussion becomes decision. None of these transformations are announced. The output reads as a coherent narrative because that is what synthesis produces. Reading for flow means reading the synthesis. Reading for claims means checking whether each factual assertion is supported by a named source.
beats.new_visual_element: Synthesis is not neutral — caveats drop / projections become results / discussion becomes decision / not announced

beats.narration_text: The brief has a field that prevents the most consequential errors: claims that must not appear. You fill it before the task runs. Do not present projections as results. Do not attribute outcomes to the program without naming the counterfactual limitation. Do not state enrollment exceeded target unless the source document uses that exact language. Your knowledge of what the project cannot yet claim goes in this field — before the task, not in a follow-up correction.
beats.new_visual_element: Claims-must-not field — your project knowledge / projections not results / attribution limits / before the task
beats.spark_line: Write what it must not claim.

beats.narration_text: Figures fail in a specific way: caption and content can diverge. A caption describes a trend the chart does not show. A table presents a calculated subtotal that does not match the row sum. Speaker notes in a deck can surface meeting-note language — including comments about personnel that were never intended for an external audience. Each requires a different check: caption against chart, table against source rows, notes against the distribution list.
beats.new_visual_element: Figures fail differently — caption vs. chart / table vs. source rows / speaker notes vs. audience

beats.narration_text: The ownership question applies to every claim in a Cowork report: could you defend this if asked? If you read for flow and approved it, you own the claims you did not verify. The verification gate — reading claim by claim against named sources — is not optional on work that carries your name. Cowork assembles. You sign.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — verification checklist / each claim + named source / figures checked / you sign it

beats.narration_text: Add the claims-must-not field before your next report task. Paste this.
beats.new_visual_element: HANDOFF — report brief with claims-must-not field

props.command: Assemble a report from these sources. Before you start — claims that must NOT appear: do not present projections as results; do not attribute outcomes without naming the data source; do not state [X] unless the source document uses that exact language. For every factual claim, note the source document and section. Flag any figure where caption and chart content could be read differently. [attach sources]

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Report Claim Tracing: Read for Evidence, Not Flow] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
